did.unittitle: Andover Newton Theological Seminary collection of Jonathan Edwards

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Sermons in Series II may be accessed only one folder at at time.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Acquired as part of the establishment of the Andover Newton Seminary at Yale Divinity School, 2017.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into six series: I. Manuscripts, circa 1650-1764, undated. II. Sermons, 1733-1758. III. Miscellaneous Papers, 1685-1714, undated. IV. Letters Regarding Edwards Manuscripts, 1824-1903. V. Additional Edwards-Related Materials, 1714-1901. VI. Andover Newton Theological Seminary Collection File, 1957-2017.

bioghist.head: Jonathan Edwards (1703-1758)
bioghist.p: Jonathan Edwards, among the foremost theologians and philosophers of his time, was born in East Windsor, Connecticut, on October 5, 1703. He attended Yale College and graduated with a Bachelor of Arts in 1720. He returned in 1723 to receive his Master of Arts degree, and served as a tutor from 1724 to 1726. Upon leaving Yale, he succeeded his grandfather, Solomon Stoddard, as minister of the Congregationalist Church at Northampton, Massachusetts from 1726 to 1750. There he became known as an evangelical preacher and stern Calvinist, helping inspire the "Great Awakening" of the 1740s. His writings, which were widely circulated, included A Faithful Narrative, Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God, Distinguishing Marks of a Work of the Spirit of God, Some Thoughts Concerning the Revivals, and A Treatise Concerning Religious Affections.

Edwards eventually became alienated from his Northampton congregation, and was dismissed after a protracted controversy over qualifications for church membership. In 1751 he became a missionary to the Mahican and Mohawk Indians at Stockbridge, Massachusetts, and minister to the English congregation there. While at Stockbridge he continued writing treatises that expounded his theology, including Freedom of the Will, Original Sin, The Nature of True Virtue, and The End for Which God Created the World. In 1757, he accepted an offer to become the president of the College of New Jersey, now Princeton University. In March 1758 he received a smallpox innoculation which proved to be infected, and died.

For more information on Edwards's life, see George M. Marsden, Jonathan Edwards: A Life (New Haven: Yale University Press, 2003); Perry Miller, Jonathan Edwards (New York: Sloan, 1949); Ola E. Winslow, Jonathan Edwards, 1703-1758: A Biography (New York: Macmillan, 1941); and Iain H. Murray, Jonathan Edwards: A New Biography (Carlisle, Penn.: Banner of Truth, 1987). Readers should also consult The Works of Jonathan Edwards (New Haven: Yale University Press, 1957-ongoing) for further definitive information on Edwards's life and writings.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: According to a 1975 address by Ellis E. O'Neal, Jr. (the typescript of which is found in the Andover Newton Theological Seminary's files concerning the collection, Series VI), the Jonathan Edwards papers arrived at Andover in 1865 when they were inherited by Elizabeth Bradford Dwight, wife of Professor Egbert C. Smyth. O'Neal writes:

"The story of the Edwards' manuscripts at Andover Theological Seminary actually begins after the publication of what is commonly referred to as the Worcester edition of Edwards' works by the Rev. Samuel Austin in 1809. President Timothy Dwight of Yale, a grandson of Edwards', was requested by his uncles to write a new life of Edwards. Apparently it was his intention to incorporate it in a definitive edition of Edwards' works. However, his death in 1817 prevented the completion of this project. In a presentation before the Massachusetts Historical Society in 1901, Franklin B. Dexter, Assistant Librarian of Yale College, stated that there was 'good evidence that President Dwight's son, Dr. Sereno E. Dwight, received from his father…an injunction to carry out the work not yet accomplished, and accordingly soon began to gather the material, securing some manuscripts by gift and copies of others… A portion of this material is believed to have been secured by Dr. Dwight at Windsor, Connecticut, the home of Edwards' boyhood.' (Franklin B. Dexter, 'the Manuscripts of Jonathan Edwards,' Massachusetts Historical Society, Proceedings, 2nd. Ser., 15 (1901, 1902): 3-4.)

This material constituted a part of the estate which Sereno Dwight, upon his death in 1850, left to his brother, the Rev. Dr. William T. Dwight. The latter Dwight, an eminent minister in Portland, Maine, bequeathed the papers to his daughter, Elizabeth Bradford Dwight Smyth, the wife of Professor Egbert C. Smyth. Smyth was the Brown Professor of Ecclesiastical History at Andover and also served as president of the faculty for approximately nineteen years. Mrs. Smyth died a few weeks before her husband so the papers came to Andover from the Professor's estate."

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

This collection remains arranged and described largely as it was received from Andover Newton Theological Seminary. It has been rehoused into standard archival containers. Fragile manuscripts in Series I, which were previously grouped together in folders by rough date span, have been rehoused into individual folders for each item. The original folder titles have been retained as headings in the list, the order of the items remains the same, and the item numbers within each grouping have been retained in the finding aid and on paper wrappers that remain with the items.

The container list from Andover Newton Theological Seminary has been reformatted with minor changes to make it compatible with Yale's systems and standards. The original container list was created by George S. Claghorn, editor of The Letters of Jonathan Edwards, when the collection was on loan to Yale between 1957 and 1974, and was likely based on an existing arrangement of the material made prior to that time. The Claghorn list was edited and augmented by staff at Andover between 1974 and 2017. This current finding aid lists, as separate series, a file of nineteenth century correspondence about the collection (Series IV), additional material not included in the Claghorn finding aid or subsequently listed by Andover staff (Series V), and the Andover Newton Theological Seminary files about the collection (Series VI). As the original finding aid contained no contextual information, Beinecke staff also added descriptive information about Edwards and the collection contents.
The following items were not found by Beinecke staff when rehousing the collection after it was received in 2017:
--1751 Aug 7. Pynchon, Joseph. ALS to Col. Ephraim Williams and JE. With letter draft by JE to Thomas Hubbard, dated August 1751 (originally listed as 1751 item 13 in Manuscripts, now Series I).
--Dwight, Sereno E. Index to Dwight's Life of JE. 50 pp., quarto (originally listed as item 9 in Miscellaneous Papers, now Series III).
Throughout the collection on the paper enclosures from Andover are notes indicating that there exist photocopies in a separate file. This file is located in Series VI, Box 20.
Scope and content notes appearing in quotes have been transcribed from the original finding aid or from the housings or notes accompanying the items.

relatedmaterial.head: Associated Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Jonathan Edwards Collection (GEN MSS 151), Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: The papers consist of writings and correspondence of and about theologian Jonathan Edwards and his family that were held by the Andover Newton Theological Seminary from the mid-nineteenth century until 2017. The collection includes sermons and other writings by Edwards, his correspondence with contemporaries, family correspondence including letters among Edwards, his wife and their children, his sisters, and his parents, and other family papers, including sermons by his father-in-law James Pierpont, who is regarded as one of the founders of Yale College. A few of the letters and other items are written on the versos of printed broadsides. The papers also include editorial work carried out by Edwards's descendants, including grandson Yale President Timothy Dwight and his sons Sereno E. Dwight and William T. Dwight, including manuscript copies of Edwards's writings chiefly made by Sereno E. Dwight, and correspondence among later Edwards descendants and Andover faculty members who had married into the Edwards family, as well as others, documenting further editorial projects.

Throughout the papers is evidence of the scholarship and editorial work by generations of Edwards descendants in the form of notes and descriptions accompanying many of the items, often on former enclosures that also bear a previous numbering system for the papers. Additional notes by Andover librarians and archivists and by editors of the Yale edition of Edwards's works are also occasionally present.

subject.part: Indians of North America -- Missions -- Massachusetts

did.unittitle: Manuscripts

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I. Manuscripts is arranged in rough chronological order, with undated material at the beginning. The material is arranged under headings which correspond to the folder groupings in which it was housed at Andover Newton Theological Seminary. Within each grouping items are numbered; this numbering system and the order of the material reflect the arrangement of the material at Andover. Dates of material in a few cases do not match the date heading under which items are filed.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series contains correspondence, notes, and other writings by Jonathan Edwards and members of his family. A few letters from others outside the family are present, including contemporary theologians and commnunity members. Throughout are later manuscript copies created by Edwards descendants, chiefly his great-grandson Sereno Edwards Dwight. In most cases the original is also present in the collection. The copies are dated, as they were in the finding aid received with the collection, for the content of the original rather than the creation of the copy.

Jonathan Edwards Autograph Sermons

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series contains sermons and sermon notes written by Jonathan Edwards. Four of the sermons listed here are not present in the series, but are represented by an empty envelope. In three of the cases the original manuscript of the sermon is not present in the collection and the original is thought not to survive; in one case the sermon is on the verso of a letter filed in Series I. For the three sermons no longer extant in the collection, the copies referred to in the listing and on the envelopes is the copy by Sereno Dwight in Series III.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged as received from Andover Newton Theological Seminary.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "(Prob. 1741-2) + Jan 1752" "(no "Doc." Theme is Esau's selling his birthright)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "(Prob. 1742)" "Christ is to the heart of a saint like a river to the root of a tree that is planted by it." "4 lvs scrap paper pinned together. Outline."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Lieutenant Kingsley's." "I. The loving kindness of God towards men through Jesus Christ is transcendently excellent. II. This great excellency is the ground on which the children of men put their trust under the shadow of God's wings. (p.1)"

did.unittitle: 4. Prov. 3:6

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "St." "If men will have respect to God and honor him in all their ways, God will make their paths straight. (p.1). (To Indians going off to fight with the English against the French)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Mar 30, 1751 + Ind. 1751" "At Sarg.Allen's." "Subject: The mourning of sinners in an other world. (p.1)"

scopecontent.p: "St. Ind. & Moh., Feb. 1752." "1. At last when wicked men come to hell they will believe that they heard in the preaching of the word…. 2. They will see what fools they were. (p.1; there are three main heads.)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Sabbath night at neighbor Allyn's, Nov., 1750 + 1751. St. Ind. & Moh., 1751." "Subject, Christ's love to those that love him. (p.1)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "use of worldly things by godly and wicked" "Doc. 1. That 'tis to the godly alone that God gives wisdom and understanding …. 2. Doc 2. That God gives wicked men the travail and vexation of gathering and keeping worldly goods…(p.4; also p. 17 slightly modified)"

did.unittitle: 9. Eccl. 4:5

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Many persons suffer the most extreme misery through a slothful neglecting to seek their own good. (p.2)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "(probably between Jan.&Mar. or Apr. 1744)" " The work of redemption ois as it were the creation of a new heaven and a new earth. (extant p. 41). (Beginning, exegesis, and one main unit lost at beginning)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "God is wont to cause hope and comfort to arise to souls after trouble and humbling for sin, and according as the troubler is slain and forsaken. (pp.6,22,61)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "That tho' there may be a very great likeness between true Christians and false professors, yet there is also a vast unlikeliness."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Stockbridge Indians, Jan. 1752" "Obs. Those that God sends forth to preach the gospel are fitly compared to the husbandman that goes forth th sew his seed. (p.1) (This sermon is probably the first in a Stockbridge series on the parable of the sower. Four others, presumably 2-5 -- are in the Yale collection.)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Jun 1737 + 1751. Part to St. Ind. & Moh., April 1752." "There are some persons that wish they had salvation and yet won't comply with the necessary means of it. (p. 5)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Lecture before sacrament, St. Ind. Jan 31, 1752" " (No 'Doc.' or headings. Incipit: 'this was the last night before X was crucified./ X knew he should be crucified the next day tho the d. did not know it…')"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "St. Ind. April, 1754." "I. The devil is an enemy that is like a strong man armed. (p.1) (There are VI heads and Application)."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "That the spiritual blessings of the gospel are fitly represented by a feast. (p.3)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Middle or late 1740s?" "I. How Christ rejoices…II. Why… III. How he expresses his joy… (p.1; this is outline of sermon)"

scopecontent.p: "St. Jonathan Rust's, March, 1750/51" "That the Lord Jesus Christ in his coming into the soul in his spiritual and blessed making(?) influences is like the rising of the day star. (p.1)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "To the Mohawks at the treaty, Aug. 16, 1751." "(Text: 'We have also a more sure word of prophecy.' Homiletically formless. Begins: 'These honourable gentelmen speak in the name…but I in the name of J.X. / When G. first made man he had a principle of holiness in his heart...')"

did.unittitle: 42. I John 3:3

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "A true hope of a state of salvation is altogether of a purifying nature in everyone that has it. (p.1)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "At Pascommuck, March 1, 1743/4 + October, 1753." "I. What sort of persons they are that Christ promises a glorious reward to… (p.1; there are IV main props.)"

did.unittitle: 44. No text (Speech to Mohawks)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Begins: 'There was a young woman whose name was Mary that lived in a town in the land of Canaan called Nazareth. She was one of the posterity of a greta king of the Jews I told you [about] whose name was David…' (p.1)" "(Defective after p. 8)"

did.unittitle: 45. No text (to Indian Children)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "To the Indian Children at Stockbridge, Feb. 1750/51." "Begins: 'There is a great God that has made heaven and earth--who has made you. And he has not made you like the beasts--given you souls--never die--go into another world…' (p.1)"

did.unittitle: 46. No text (Speech to Mohawks)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Probably January, 1751." "Heading: 'Speech to the Mohawks.' (really a sort of sermon)" "Begins: 'I bid you all wellcome to N.E. I am glad to see you. "Your coming here will rejoyce the hearts of all good men as they hope it will be a means of your coming into greater light and knowledge in the Xtian religion..." ' (p.1)"

Andover Newton Theological Seminary Collection File

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged as received from Andover Newton Theological Seminary, with the exception that the nineteenth-century correspondence about the collection orginially filed here has been removed to create a separate series (Series IV).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of information about the collection kept as part of the Andover Newton Theological Seminary Library's files. It includes the paper finding aids on which this present finding aid is based and correspondence documenting the work of Yale editors with the collection.
